meta.purpose: EXP-050-numerics: Verified cascade numerical claims (B4 blocker)

claim_1_refraction_angle.corrected_range: 6.3-29.0 degrees (full range); 13.4-27.4 degrees at typical observer distances (5000-15000 km) during winter solstice
claim_1_refraction_angle.note: Original claim of 13-27 deg is accurate for winter at mid-range distances. Summer requires 6-13 deg. Both vastly exceed real atmospheric refraction (0.57 deg max at horizon).

claim_2_ratio_vs_atmospheric.original_claim: 400-675x the n(r) value the dome formula yields for starlight
claim_2_ratio_vs_atmospheric.corrected: The ratio of cascade-required bending to real atmospheric refraction is 11-51x vs horizon maximum (0.57 deg), or 210-967x vs zenith refraction (0.03 deg). The original 400-675x claim conflated two different comparisons. Recommend stating: cascade requires 11-51x the strongest known atmospheric refraction (horizon), or 200-1000x typical zenith refraction.

claim_3_nr_inconsistency.original_claim: n(r) produces 0.04 deg bending for starlight but cascade needs 13-27 deg for sunlight (400-675x ratio)
claim_3_nr_inconsistency.corrected: The n(r) formula produces LARGE refractive indices (1.05 to 2.84 across the disc). At r=6700km (sun orbit), n=1.235. This does NOT produce 0.04 deg starlight bending — it implies multi-degree bending for ANY ray traversing a medium with these n(r) values. The real inconsistency is: (a) if n(r) is this large, ALL observed star positions should be displaced by degrees, contradicting Gaia microarcsecond precision; (b) if n(r) is small enough for Gaia consistency (~1.0000001), it cannot bend the sun by 13-27 deg. The dome cannot have both.
claim_3_nr_inconsistency.structural_argument: n(r) >= 1.2 at the sun orbit radius would cause multi-degree displacement of all stellar positions at that radius. Gaia DR3 measures stellar parallaxes to ~10 microarcseconds (0.00000278 deg). For n(r) to be consistent with Gaia, (n-1) must be < ~1e-7 at relevant radii. The dome formula gives n-1 = 0.235 at r=6700km — eight orders of magnitude too large for stellar astrometry, and simultaneously too small to produce the 13-27 deg solar bending needed (which requires careful path integration through a graded medium, not a simple n-1 estimate).
claim_3_nr_inconsistency.note: A proper ray-trace through the dome n(r) field is needed for a precise starlight bending figure. The qualitative argument is clear: n(r) values of 1.2-2.8 are catastrophically inconsistent with microarcsecond stellar astrometry.

claim_4_angular_diameter_modulation.note: If physical sun altitude varies 1975-3618 km, and the sun maintains constant physical size, its angular diameter as seen from below varies by 83.2%. This is 24x larger than the observed 3.4% seasonal variation. Not observed.

claim_5_tidal_modulation.note: Tidal force varies as 1/r^3. Physical altitude ratio of 1.83x gives 6.1x tidal force variation. Original claim of 3-8x brackets the computed value. Real seasonal solar tidal variation is ~6% from eccentricity. The dome cascade predicts 100x the observed seasonal variation.
